what_it_does: Defines a SEED form recognizer that identifies log-shaped units—changelogs, handoffs, session/build logs, or status notes—by looking for log keywords in the first five lines or at least three timestamped lines, and routes matching units to the cheap legibility stage using the prose_default policy.
observations: The docstring frames logs as bookkeeping that should not receive deep capture effort.
_match first scans the first five lines for changelog/handoff/session log/build log/status, then counts lines starting with an ISO-like date.
The FORM dictionary exposes id, match callable, stage, policy, and a short desc.
The file stem 'log' is required to equal the form id.
The meta parameter in _match is accepted but unused.
standouts: Explicitly routes logs to the cheap/skip band to avoid burning capture depth.
Uses a hard threshold of three timestamped lines to decide a text is log-shaped.
Keyword detection is restricted to the first five lines.
conventions: Follows the FORM registry pattern with a dict containing id, match, stage, policy, desc.
Uses from __future__ annotations and type hints.
Docstring doubles as a design rationale note.
concerns: The timestamp regex only matches ISO dates beginning with a four-digit year, so other common log date formats may be missed.
Keyword detection is limited to the first five lines, which could miss log-shaped content where keywords appear later.
The meta argument is part of the signature but ignored.
The threshold of three timestamped lines is arbitrary, though documented.
notes: A deliberately lightweight log detector that classifies common timestamped or keyword-tagged bookkeeping text and sends it to the cheapest processing band.
questions: How is this form registered and invoked by runtime/forms.py?
What does the prose_default policy specify?
Is the meta argument reserved for future context, or should it influence matching?
Why was three timestamped lines chosen as the threshold?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the implementation: it recognizes log-shaped units and routes them to the legibility stage with the prose_default policy.
apparent_themes: Cheap-band processing for high-volume bookkeeping content
intention_signals: Encode a simple, fast classification rule that says logs/changelogs/handoffs are abundant and low-yield for deep analysis, so they should be recognized early and handled by the cheapest available pass.
novelty: It bakes a value judgment about capture depth directly into a form recognizer, treating log-shaped content as a class to be under-captured rather than richly parsed.
contribution: A reusable, low-cost log detector that prevents over-investment in log/changelog/handoff content by binding it to a cheap prose policy and a legibility stage.
summary_for_embedding: company/forms/log.py defines a SEED form recognizer that identifies log-shaped text via timestamped lines or log keywords and routes matches to the legibility stage under the prose_default policy.
intention_for_embedding: It was meant to cheaply classify common bookkeeping logs, changelogs, and handoffs so the system does not waste deep capture effort on them.

symbols.does: Detects log-shaped text by log keywords in the first five lines or by counting timestamped lines.
symbols.description: _match takes a text string and an optional meta dict, builds a five-line head, checks it against a regex of log-related words, and if no keyword matches it counts lines that begin with an ISO-like date; it returns true when three or more such timestamped lines are found. The function is the matching predicate used by the FORM registry entry.
